solution: Apply \( f \) repeatedly starting from \( x_{0} = [[x0]] \). After [[n]] applications of \( f(x) = [[a]]x + ([[b]]) \), the value is \( x_{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This applies the multiplier \( [[a]]^{[[n]]} \) but forgets the added constants accumulated at each step.
This scales \( x_0 \) correctly but adds \( [[b]] \) only [[n]] times instead of compounding it.
This applies \( f \) once and then just adds \( [[b]] \) repeatedly, ignoring the compounding of the multiplier.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x + ([[b]]) \). What value of \( x \) satisfies \( f(x) = x \) (a fixed point of \( f \))?
distractor_rationales: This sets the fixed-point equation up as \( ax = b \), forgetting to subtract \( x \) from both sides first.
This flips the sign in the denominator, using \( a+1 \) instead of \( a - 1 \).
This forgets to negate the numerator when isolating \( x \).
This negates the correct value (a sign slip when isolating \( x \)).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This stops after one application of \( f \) instead of two.
This squares the entire first result INCLUDING re-adding \( c \) incorrectly inside the square, rather than applying \( f \) properly a second time.
This combines both squarings into a single quadruple power, skipping the intermediate addition of \( c \) after the first step.
This applies the squaring only once but adds \( c \) twice.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = x^{2} + ([[c]]) \). Which of the following is a fixed point of \( f \) (a solution of \( f(x) = x \))?
solution: A fixed point satisfies \( x^{2} + ([[c]]) = x \), i.e. \( x^{2} - x + ([[c]]) = 0 \). By the quadratic formula, \( x = \dfrac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 4([[c]])}}{2} \); one such fixed point is \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the constant \( c \) itself rather than solving the fixed-point equation.
This reports the negative of \( c \) rather than solving the fixed-point equation.
This uses the sum of the two fixed points (which equals 1) minus \( c \), not one of the fixed points itself.
This is off by one from a correct fixed point.
This is off by one from a correct fixed point.
This is off by two from a correct fixed point.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \). This function has two real fixed points (solutions of \( f(x) = x \)). What is their sum?
solution: Fixed points satisfy \( [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) = x \), i.e. \( [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]]-1)x + ([[c]]) = 0 \). By Vieta's formulas, the sum of the roots of this quadratic is \( -\dfrac{[[b]]-1}{[[a]]} = \dfrac{1-[[b]]}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to negate the shifted linear coefficient \( (b-1) \) before dividing by \( a \).
This computes the correct sum but adds an extra 1.
This forgets to shift the linear coefficient by \( -1 \) before applying Vieta's formula.
This uses the coefficients as if finding the roots of \( f(x) = 0 \) rather than the fixed-point equation \( f(x) = x \).
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.

solution: Since \( f(f(x)) = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[c]]/x} = x \), applying \( f \) twice returns the original value: \( f \) has period 2. So \( x_{[[n]]} \) equals \( x_0 \) if [[n]] is even, and \( f(x_0) = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[x0]]} \) if [[n]] is odd. Here \( x_{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes [[n]] is even when it is actually odd (or vice versa), giving \( x_0 \) unchanged instead of applying \( f \) once more.
This assumes [[n]] is odd when it is actually even (or vice versa), applying \( f \) once when zero (net) applications are needed.
This negates \( x_0 \), which has no basis in the period-2 behavior of \( f \).
This negates \( f(x_0) \), which has no basis in the period-2 behavior of \( f \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
